title: Alternative Investment Qualification Rule
content:
This document establishes Meridian Wealth Partners' qualification requirements for recommending alternative investment products to clients, ensuring compliance with SEC Regulation D 506(b) and FINRA Rule 2111 suitability obligations.

ALTERNATIVE INVESTMENT SCOPE
This rule applies to all alternative investments including private equity funds, hedge funds, real estate investment trusts (REITs), commodity funds, structured products, and private placement offerings under Regulation D. These investments carry enhanced risk profiles and liquidity constraints requiring specialized knowledge and client assessment protocols.

CLIENT QUALIFICATION CRITERIA
Clients must meet both accredited investor standards and Meridian's internal suitability requirements. Accredited investor status requires either: (1) individual net worth exceeding $1,000,000 excluding primary residence, or (2) annual income of $200,000 ($300,000 joint) in each of the two most recent years with reasonable expectation of same income level in current year.

Additional Meridian requirements include: minimum liquid net worth of $500,000 beyond the proposed alternative investment amount, demonstrated investment sophistication through prior alternative investment experience or professional credentials, and ability to bear total loss of investment without materially affecting lifestyle or retirement security.

PORTFOLIO ALLOCATION LIMITS
Alternative investments shall not exceed 15% of client's total investable assets for clients with net worth between $1-3 million. For clients exceeding $3 million net worth, allocation may reach 25% with documented risk tolerance assessment. Ultra-high net worth clients ($10+ million) may allocate up to 35% with appropriate diversification across alternative asset classes.

DUE DILIGENCE REQUIREMENTS
Senior advisors must complete enhanced due diligence including: review of private placement memorandum, analysis of management team track record, assessment of fee structures including carried interest arrangements, evaluation of liquidity terms and redemption restrictions, and documentation of correlation benefits within client's overall portfolio.

For hedge funds, minimum requirements include three-year operational history, assets under management exceeding $100 million, independent administrator and auditor, and institutional-quality risk management systems. Private equity investments require minimum fund size of $250 million and established general partner with successful prior fund performance.

CLIENT DISCLOSURE AND DOCUMENTATION
Prior to any alternative investment recommendation, advisors must provide comprehensive risk disclosure covering: illiquidity risk with typical holding periods of 3-10 years, higher fee structures averaging 2% management fees plus 20% performance fees, limited regulatory oversight compared to registered securities, potential for total capital loss, and tax implications including K-1 reporting requirements.

Client acknowledgment forms must document understanding of: investment objectives and strategy, fee structure impact on returns, liquidity restrictions and capital call requirements for private equity, correlation with traditional assets during market stress periods, and concentration risk if multiple alternative investments are held.

COMPLIANCE MONITORING
All alternative investment recommendations require pre-approval from the Alternative Investment Committee comprising senior portfolio managers and compliance officers. Post-investment monitoring includes quarterly performance reporting, annual suitability reviews confirming continued appropriateness, and immediate notification requirements for material changes in fund management or strategy.

Advisors must maintain current CAIA designation or complete annual alternative investment training modules. This rule supersedes all previous alternative investment guidelines and requires immediate implementation across all client relationships.
